questions.text: Who is the author of the lesson 'Indigo'?
questions.explanation: Louis Fischer, an American journalist and biographer of Gandhi, wrote 'Indigo'.

questions.text: The lesson 'Indigo' is an excerpt from which book?
questions.explanation: It is taken from Fischer's famous biography titled 'The Life of Mahatma Gandhi'.

questions.text: In which year did the incident of Champaran take place?
questions.explanation: The Champaran Satyagraha, Gandhi's first major civil disobedience movement in India, occurred in 1917.

questions.text: Who was Rajkumar Shukla?
questions.explanation: Rajkumar Shukla was a determined peasant from Champaran who sought Gandhi's help.

questions.text: Why did Rajkumar Shukla want to meet Gandhi?
options: To invite him to a wedding
To complain about the injustice of the landlord system
questions.explanation: He wanted Gandhi to address the grievances of the sharecroppers in Champaran.

questions.text: Where did the annual convention of the Indian National Congress take place in 1916?
questions.explanation: Shukla first approached Gandhi at the Lucknow convention in December 1916.

questions.text: What did the British landlords force the peasants to grow on 15% of their land?
questions.explanation: The long-term contract required peasants to plant indigo on 15% of their holdings.

questions.text: What was the 'bait' or the problem for the British landlords regarding Indigo later on?
questions.explanation: Synthetic indigo from Germany made natural indigo cultivation unprofitable for the landlords.

questions.text: Gandhi's first visit with Rajkumar Shukla in Bihar was to the house of:
questions.explanation: Shukla took Gandhi to Patna, to the house of a lawyer named Rajendra Prasad, who later became the President of India.

questions.text: Why did the servants at Rajendra Prasad's house mistake Gandhi for another peasant?
options: He was with Shukla and dressed simply
questions.explanation: Gandhi's simple appearance and the company of Shukla led the servants to assume he was just another sharecropper.

questions.text: Why was Gandhi not allowed to draw water from the well at Rajendra Prasad's house?
options: They feared he would pollute the source (untouchability)
questions.explanation: The servants were not sure if Gandhi was an untouchable and didn't want him to pollute the entire well.

questions.text: Gandhi went to Muzzafarpur first to:
options: Get more complete information than Shukla could give
questions.explanation: He wanted to gather facts and details from the local lawyers and scholars.

questions.text: Who received Gandhi at the Muzzafarpur railway station?
questions.explanation: Professor J.B. Kripalani of the Arts College in Muzzafarpur received him with a large body of students.

questions.text: Why did Gandhi chide the lawyers of Muzzafarpur?
options: For charging high fees from the poor peasants
questions.explanation: Gandhi believed it was shameful to take large fees from the exploited and terror-stricken peasants.

questions.text: What, according to Gandhi, was more important than taking cases to court?
questions.explanation: Gandhi felt that law courts were useless as long as the peasants were crushed and fear-stricken.

questions.text: How did the British landlords react when they realized synthetic indigo was available?
options: They asked for compensation to release peasants from the 15% agreement
questions.explanation: They tried to extort money from the peasants as 'compensation' for ending the old contract.

questions.text: When Gandhi reached Champaran, what was the first thing he did?
options: Tried to get the facts from the Secretary of the British Landlords' Association
questions.explanation: He sought information from official sources before starting any action.

questions.text: How did the British Commissioner of Tirhut division treat Gandhi?
options: He bullied him and advised him to leave Tirhut
questions.explanation: The official was hostile and ordered Gandhi to leave the district immediately.

questions.text: Instead of leaving Tirhut, where did Gandhi go?
questions.explanation: Motihari was the capital of Champaran, and he continued his investigation there.

questions.text: Why was Gandhi served an official notice to quit Champaran?
options: Because his presence was causing trouble
questions.explanation: The British authorities viewed his inquiry as a threat to their authority and peace.

questions.text: When Gandhi refused to obey the order to leave, what happened the next day?
options: He was summoned to appear in court
questions.explanation: He was asked to appear in the Motihari court for disobeying the government order.

questions.text: What was the result of thousands of peasants gathering around the courthouse in Motihari?
options: The officials felt powerless and the trial was postponed
Gandhi was sent to jail immediately
questions.explanation: The 'spontaneous demonstration' was the beginning of their liberation from the fear of the British.

questions.text: Gandhi told the court that he was involved in a 'conflict of duties'. What were they?
options: Being a lawyer vs being a politician
questions.explanation: He didn't want to set a bad example as a law-breaker, but he had to obey the higher law of conscience.

questions.text: What did the prominent lawyers decide to do if Gandhi was sent to jail?
options: Fight the case for the landlords
questions.explanation: Initially they wanted to leave, but Gandhi's willingness to go to jail for strangers moved them to join him.

questions.text: When the Lieutenant-Governor dropped the case against Gandhi, it was the first triumph of:
questions.explanation: Gandhi and the lawyers had successfully challenged an unjust law through non-violent means.

questions.text: What percentage of refund did the landlords eventually offer to the peasants?
questions.explanation: They offered 25%, thinking Gandhi would demand 50% and they could block the deal.

questions.text: Why did Gandhi agree to a 25% refund when the peasants were entitled to more?
options: The amount was less important than the landlords' loss of prestige
He was afraid of the landlords
questions.explanation: For Gandhi, the surrender of the landlords' prestige and money was a psychological victory for the peasants.

questions.text: What health issues did Gandhi address in Champaran?
options: Miserable health conditions and lack of sanitation
questions.explanation: Gandhi noticed the filthy state of clothes and villages and called for volunteers for medical and educational help.

questions.text: Who volunteered to teach in the primary schools opened in Champaran villages?
questions.explanation: Two young men who had just become disciples of Gandhi, along with their wives, volunteered.

questions.text: Who was Charles Freer Andrews?
options: A pacifist and devoted follower of Gandhi
questions.explanation: He was an English pacifist who became a close associate of Gandhi.

questions.text: Why did Gandhi refuse the lawyers' help to keep Charles Freer Andrews in Champaran?
questions.explanation: Gandhi wanted the lawyers to rely on their own strength rather than looking for a 'prop' in an Englishman.

questions.text: What were the three medicines available in the Champaran medical camp?
questions.explanation: These simple medicines were used to treat a variety of common ailments in the village.

questions.text: How long did Gandhi stay in Champaran for the initial visit?
questions.explanation: The initial uninterrupted stay lasted for seven months, followed by several shorter visits.

questions.text: Champaran satyagraha was not just a political movement; it was also a:
questions.explanation: Gandhi worked to remove the cultural and social backwardness of the Champaran villages.

questions.text: The theme of 'Indigo' centers around:
options: The struggle of the underprivileged against oppression
questions.explanation: It highlights how leadership and non-violence can empower the poor to fight for their rights.

questions.text: What did Gandhi tell his wife Kasturba to do in the villages?
options: Talk to women about personal cleanliness
questions.explanation: She was asked to teach the women hygiene and how to keep their surroundings clean.

questions.text: How did Gandhi describe the Champaran episode?
options: As a turning point in his life
questions.explanation: He saw it as a moment where he realized the British could not order him around in his own country.

questions.text: What did 'Civil Disobedience' look like to the British?
options: A challenge to their moral authority
questions.explanation: It showed that the power of truth and non-violence was stronger than the might of the Empire.

questions.text: Which attribute of Gandhi is most highlighted in this lesson?
questions.explanation: Gandhi's ability to combine high ideals with practical, day-to-day problems of the people is central to the story.

questions.text: What did the Champaran episode prove to the peasants?
options: That they had rights and defenders
That Indigo was a good crop
questions.explanation: It gave them the courage to stand up and taught them that they had legal and moral rights.
